speaker: interviewer
text: With Mr. William Cohn, Dallas, Texas, United States of America. Mr.  Cohn, you were talking about receiving a postcard from your mother while you were in North Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After the Crystal Night, the-- our landlord, who had made an agreement with my father to open up a big store, he cancelled the lease for our apartment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents had to move out [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and into-- into one room with another Jewish couple-- a-- a couple where the husband was a Russian Jew that was a prisoner of war in Germany and, had, after the war, remained in Germany and married a non-Jewish woman. And he had a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he gave one room to my-- to my parents. And in that house, my father died a year after-- a little over a year after he was released from the concentration camp. And then, after that, my mother was forced, again, to move out there. And I-- I imagine, because she was left alone, that she was not allowed to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to live in a room like that. I don't know. Anyway, she was forced again, but not by the people who owned the house. Because they were-- he was Jewish, you know. But for some reason, she had to-- she was forced, again, to move out. And she moved into a dingy-- dingy basement apartment-- not apartment, into a room, basement room of a house that belonged to a-- a Jewish shoemaker. And-- and there-- there she lived. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and-- there was the president of the Jewish community. He adored my mother, always. And after my father had died, they moved together in that-- in that room, in the basement room. And from then on, I-- I didn't get any more letters. Yes, I did get letters. Yeah. Because, from 1938 to '39, I still get postcards. They stopped writing letters, because-- and they started writing postcards because they figured that, on postcards, they could write more openly because a censor wouldn't think-- wouldn't think to-- to that people would write anything objectionable, you know, dangerous, on a postcard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these postcards are really more explicit and-- about the situation than the letters were. And I still have those postcards. And I-- and I gave it to the Holocaust Center and translated it. But-- and it's really-- it was very painful to translate. I didn't expect it to be, but it was. And from those postcards, I learned also a lot. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the when the war broke out, September 1939, and then in October 19, I joined the British Army. And met-- so I had-- had no more address in Haifa. So my-- so my mother didn't know where to-- where to address these postcards. So she addressed them to my uncle Tel Aviv. And he, in turn, then gave-- somehow-- somehow got them to me, you know? When I was on leave, whenever there was a leave or something. This last one I got, I got through the military mail. Yeah. And so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I learned about my mother living in my hometown in Stendel until she was deported that she was with friends, friends or customers, not Jews-- non-- non-Jews took care of her. You know, Jews weren't not even allowed anymore to go into food stores and buy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So friends of my family yes, and old customers brought her-- brought her food. Then my cousins, after the-- [INAUDIBLE] letters to my-- to my-- to my sister with my-- these two cousins in Berlin. They [? have ?] Jewish cousins. They-- they all wrote about my mother, what happened to her. And they wrote that, as long as she was in Stendel, she was taken care of by friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, after the war, I got a-- got a very densely written postcard from another non-Jewish woman that was a friend of our family. And she wrote the same thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I got-- I got four [INAUDIBLE] postcards. And one of them, my mother writes that my sister was trying to get a visa for her for some small country, to buy a visa, you know, you had to buy these visas. So the-- so on one of those postcards my mother writes a-- a-- a visa for Cuba is pending. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and then the war broke out, and no more ships [INAUDIBLE] Germany. Eventually, the [INAUDIBLE], the visa came. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one postcard is where she writes that my father had died, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the other postcard-- the third postcard-- is where she writes that the change of address is imminent. She-- yeah. And it was the last postcard, which I got in El-Alamein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's from Warsaw, from the ghetto, where she writes even the address, in the [? hall. ?] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that, when she was in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '42. Yeah. And that was the last what I-- what I heard of-- from her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And always she writes how she longs for us, for my sister and for me. And she always hoped that we would see each other again, except in the one letter, in one postcard before the deportation, where she wrote that she expects a change in address. There she-- there she wrote this feeling that she wouldn't see us anymore. She wouldn't see us anymore. I'll read you the card afterwards, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, it was the fate of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I always fear that I always, the rest of my life, left with a guilt feeling that I left my mother alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'd rather have shared her fate. You know? And-- and that's why [? I'd-- ?] I'd rather have stayed with my mother until the end. That much I didn't know either, you know? But that would have [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Were you able to maintain any other communication with any other family back in Germany when you were in service?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. All our family left, was the extended family. Was not very close, except for those two cousins in Berlin. And they had to be careful, you know, to-- to lay low. So-- and-- see-- see, one was a nurse, and she continued working as a nurse. And-- and the other one, she was working in industry. She was-- she got-- the telephone in Germany is run by the government. It's not a private enterprise. It's not a private institution. So she worked for the telephone in Berlin. And she was-- she was instrumental in-- in-- in starting the self-dialing. You know. Before that, you had to call the operator and ask for a number. And then they started self-dialing. And she was-- she was very active in this, in-- in the beginning of this new system. So-- and then she worked for-- for a big industrial conglomerate-- Siemens. Siemens in [INAUDIBLE], very famous. And-- and somehow she worked also for-- for-- for industry, but always under the-- she must have had some kind of a protection by some higher ups that she thought she could survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alice and Lotta. The nurse was Lotta, and the other one was Alice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schloss. My mother brother-- you know, my mother's maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the British Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend all that time in Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Italy. [BACKGROUND NOISE] We came-- we came with him in-- we came to Italy with the invasion in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drive-- we were driving trucks. We were a company of-- of trucks-- truckers, you know, a transportation company. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One time, and I worked-- I [? went ?] a-- a machine there for anti-aircraft in Tobruk. There was-- see, in Tobruk we had-- it was at a time of [INAUDIBLE]. And the British, we are-- we were driven out of Crete by the Germans. And Churchill had sent all the armaments to Greece and had neglected North Africa. So [INAUDIBLE]-- the-- the British Army [INAUDIBLE] the Italians from North Africa and had taken Tobruk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- and the army was left with only small amounts of equipment. So in-- in Tobruk, when Tobruk, was taken, mainly by Australians, they took out the mines. Tobruk was surrounded by mines, the minefields. And the Australians got out of the mines and-- and demobilized them, yeah. And I worked with them with my truck. You know. And [INAUDIBLE]. And the Germans-- Germans returned and advanced again and-- and-- and laid siege to Trobuk. They took the same mines, the Italian mines, and put them back at the same place. And then there were-- the-- on the aircraft, the [? auto ?] was used, the [INAUDIBLE], the original [INAUDIBLE] on the aircraft was partly used. It was frequently attacked by-- by airplanes by German [INAUDIBLE]. And so there-- there was one gun on the port, a big double-barreled Fiat gun. It was manned by-- by a group of Scotsmen. And it was attacked by planes. They came out from the sun and attacked them. But it was so clumsy, that gun, so when the-- the planes turned around, and then came and attacked them from the back, because they-- they couldn't work that big gun so quick, you know. It was too slow to turn around. So if they wanted [INAUDIBLE] protect from the rear, so the [INAUDIBLE]. We stayed [INAUDIBLE] close to them. So they asked us for volunteers to protect their back, to protect their rear. So I volunteered for that [INAUDIBLE] gun. We had a-- we had a Lewis gun, a machine gun, a British machine gun. And we took that, and we-- we took a position next to that. And-- and-- and watched their rear. And one time we shot down-- shot down a-- a German plane. I didn't shoot it. But there was a sergeant from our-- our company, my company, and he shot it. But I-- I served the magazines. I filled the magazines. And, at that time, we were [? technically ?] about 60-- 60-- 60 planes. And we had about 13-- 13 bombs right next to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the-- the rest of my company, the fellows, they saw that, from the [? body ?] where they stayed, and they thought we were already gone, we were dead, you know, killed because it was all surrounded by-- by-- by the explosions, by the dust, you know? And then, when the dust cleared, they waved to  us, they saw us-- they saw us, and I-- they waved to us. So-- yeah, that was it. Anyway so, how did we get there? Oh, what did I do in the army. Otherwise, we were driving trucks. And then, yeah, yeah, we went to the invasion, to [INAUDIBLE]. But before that, yeah, when the Germans-- when Germans were driven out of Africa, North Africa, and went-- we went-- we went forward with the army, with the, you know, the rest of the army. And then we came to a halt, and we were pulled back into Benghazi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- my-- my shoulder was at the point that I couldn't do anything anymore with it. I couldn't even pull-- pull on the overcoat the army wore. So I thought, this is a good time to-- to have this-- to have this repaired, you know, if it is possible. And I thought-- see sometimes, I had these kind of foresights. Same thing that I said to my father, we can't live there anymore. And here again, I thought this was a time that an invasion started. I thought, OK, [INAUDIBLE]. Let's say we-- we would be shipped to an invasion-- to an invasion. And maybe the ship-- ship would be torpedoed or-- or attacked by a plane and-- and went down, with my shoulder, I would already be-- be lost. You know, I couldn't swim with that shoulder. So I said, this is a good time to go and have it fixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me by hospital-- hospital ship back to Cairo. And there, I was operated upon. And then, after the operation, I was sent to a-- to a recuperation camp in Alexandria, near Alexandria, Egypt. And-- and there was a Jewish soldier [? stuck ?] there. And-- and I was already better, you know. I could already move. I went to the Jewish soldier, [INAUDIBLE] this friend, was another friend there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, one day, my company came up to Alexandria, and came-- some of my friends came to the-- we met at the [INAUDIBLE]. And we were talking about being shipped to-- being shipped to-- to Italy for the-- for-- for the invasion. And we were talking openly about that, even when they are going, and so [? forth. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to them, how can you talk about that, I said, openly? All Alexandria knows already that-- that we-- my company is going on an invasion, and there are spies all over. How can you talk about it openly like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and sure enough, they went. And one morning, I woke up in the [? operation ?] camp. And-- and [INAUDIBLE] one of my guys that was with me from my company, he came into my tent and said, Willy, 462. [INAUDIBLE]. And in the sea, the Mediterranean, we were torpedoed, the ship sank, and half of my company was lost. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 148 people were drowned in-- close to Benghazi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, I had-- I had that foresight two times. You know? And [INAUDIBLE] of what happened to me, you know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, when they came back to Egypt after the-- after that-- after that disaster, and the company was reformed, and-- and my company come on-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very popular in my company. And even my-- even the officers, although I was only a corporal, you know. And my company commander [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] requested that I be transferred back to his company. And when I came to a company, he was-- I was called to his office. And he said how happy he was to have me back. [LAUGHS] You know. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, that's enough of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy, in Florence. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help liberate Florence? Did you help liberate Florence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We helped in Salerno, the first-- the first invasion in Italy. You know, first-- [INAUDIBLE] army on the Montgomery, which where we-- which where we-- officially we were part in North Africa, but, at that time, Montgomery invaded Sicily. And then the American Fifth Army invaded Salerno. And we were then detached to the Fifth Army, to the American Fifth Army for the invasion-- at the invasion of Salerno. And then, when Salerno was liberated, then we stayed in Salerno for ten months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what part of the British Army were you part of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The RASC, Royal Army Service Corps, this transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were demobilized. The-- the whole company was-- were traveled on to Italy, down to Salisbury and waited for a ship to bring us back to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find work in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I found work with the army again, as a-- as a-- as a mechanic, but not for long. I gave it up, and I joined-- I joined a-- a transport co-operative of ex-soldiers. But before that, but-- as soon as I came back to Palestine, I got married to a-- to a girl that met-- met when I  was a recruit in the Army, the first-- first months, you know. And we stayed in-- in contact. Whenever I got-- whenever I went on leave, I got back to-- to Palestine during the service, I visited her. And I visited-- and I-- then we corresponded. And then, you know, when-- when I came back, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name? Her name? My wife's name? Bronya. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was from Romania. She was actually born in Vienna, but her family was from Romania, and she grew up in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did her-- when did she and her family go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family, no-- also-- yes, her two sisters immigrated to Palestine, and her brother also. And her brother was a-- became a kibbutznik and married. And her sisters got also married, but they stayed in the city. And-- but her parents did not survive, like-- like my parents. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughters names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dorit and Delilah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have two-- two grandsons from Delilah and one granddaughter from Dorit. And her name is Rebecca.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandsons names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandsons names are David and Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you put your life back together when you came back to Palestine after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life together?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I'm sorry.
